issue_title: Accept or explicitly reject classes derived from BaseException for retrying and py27 compatibility?
issue_body:
First of all, thank you @jd and other contributors for maintaining tenacity, and making retrying easy.

Since the `tenacity` is the fork of `retrying`, a lot of code bases would want to upgrade/port from `retrying`, aside from a few common issues such as the ones pointed out in [this report](https://bugs.launchpad.net/python-oneviewclient/+bug/1635401), I ran into some compatibility issues:

https://github.com/jd/tenacity/blob/69cf4a74549b0820e6baaa188d0e72ced5ed58ad/tenacity/__init__.py#L297-L302

If I'm not mistaken, this part catches the exception thrown by the retrying function; `except Exception` is normally fine with catching all exceptions; however, when the retrying function uses a custom exception that inherits anything that's part of the `BaseException` (e.g., `SystemExit`, or just a class that inherits `BaseException`), `tenacity` will ignore any and all retrying settings and break: even if it is a bare `@retry` decorator without any arguments. This behaviour is introduced only in `tenacity`, because `retrying` library uses a bare `except:`, which catches *all* exceptions, and continues to retry.

Maybe consider adding a `except BaseException:` clause underneath the `except Exception` clause, and in that block, either accept or explicitly reject BaseException(s)? To the very least, documenting and warning about this issue seems worthy.

issue_title: See what can be borrowed from similar projects
issue_body: Initial projects to look into are [messytables](https://github.com/reubano/messytables) and [agate](https://github.com/onyxfish/agate).

## plan
1. transplant part of code from redis-py-cluster to aredis
2. make unit test and function test passed
3. optimize part of command

## Shared Array(s)
### Pros
 - Read function is trivial (1 copy from shared to local array, 1 copy/view when removing `np.nan`s)
 - Arbitrarily large arrays
### Cons
 - More complex implementation
   - Some amount of bookkeeping is required (e.g. array dimensions, yet-to-be-filled vals)
 - Less flexible data structures
    - No named data elements (why not, though?)
    - Only numpy arrays (though we can manage several arrays of different shapes)
 - Less straightforward for users to implement their own devices
    - Bookkeeping related to where data should land, ...

## Pipes
### Pros
 - Very straightforward implementation (~100 lines)
 - Straightforward to send any data structure (e.g. dict, list, array, ...)
 - Naturally handles variable amount of data per read period
 - No need to use locks when accessing data (in fact, leads to diminished performance)
### Cons
 - Fairly small buffer size limits (~65536 bytes, and could vary by platform). 
    - Exceeding this limit leads to blocking the remote process, while it waits for the pipe to free up some space (or just fails).
    - By default, Windows uses a smaller buffer size. We can encourage it to use a larger one (added in bf6ca24fac3067b6a125382d39b6d861d2c97200), but unsure if there will be any undesirable side-effects.
    - This could limit high-frequency devices (e.g. EEG)? At the least, the pipe should be emptied frequently in these cases (i.e. every framebuffer flip).
 - Read function takes non-trivial amount of time (expect worst-case 2-3 ms? Only tested on my laptop). It scales with number of elements in the buffer. Could we do magic to gradually shuffle data from the pipe to the main process via a thread when the number of elements exceeds some threshold?

issue_body:

 File "myproject/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/apitools/base/protorpclite/messages.py", line 1168, in <module>
    class Field(six.with_metaclass(_FieldMeta, object)):

issue_title: Add ability to switch between "develop" locations (often local) for a dependency and the preferred (often remote) location
issue_body: The idea would be you could optionally specify a `develop` option for dependency and then, when `--develop` is passed on the command line, or read from local configuration, that location would then be used. This really only allows seamless switching between the `develop` and preferred `location` and the use case is really to allow you to develop a lot of inter-dependent repositories at the same time and switch between local and remote code. You can achieve this today be simply locally overriding the the relevant `--<dependency>-location` setting and saving in your local conf file, but this feature should make things a little easier and allow a default `develop` path to be specified in your `scontruct` file.

issue_title: Functionality to keep connections open with ResuableStrategy
issue_body: Does the reusable strategy support sending TCP keep-alives on pooled connections to prevent server side closures? The hope is to keep a minimum number of connections open and bound in the pool. Low traffic volumes cause connections to close and slowness as the pool is rebuilt.

My understanding is that object bounding boxes need to be accurate for landmark training.

I want to train a fresh set of facial landmarks, but dlib already has good face detectors (especially the DNN one!) so I don't want to create bounding boxes manually in imglab, then label the landmarks.

To create the training data, is there:
a) A benefit to using the DNN to detect the face, then manually landmarking within the box (ie ensuring consistent object detection) ?
b) If so, is there any way to do this at the moment, or do I need to recode imglab to autodetect the bounding boxes?

I just want to check that what I am suggesting is sensible?

issue_title: is there any manual or tutorial code for pypcd
issue_body:
hi. thanks for your works. 

is there any manual or tutorial code for pypcd

Hi!
I have problems polling the SNMP table `CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2Table`. The `ObjectType` instance seems to be malformed when the object identifier contains ipv6 data (i.e. `InetAddressType` is `'ipv6'` and `InetAddress` contains a ipv6 address). That leads to some kind of Exception in prettyPrint() (see below).

The error occurs on the latest pysnmp 4.3.9, but not on 4.3.5 (with pyasn1==0.2.3). I think it was introduced in pysnmp 4.3.6, but i'm not sure.

I attached an example script performing a simple bulkwalk on `CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt`. A minimal `public.snmp_rec` file for "snmpsim" and the corresponding MIB file are also attached. [bgp-example.bulkwalk.zip](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/files/1214829/bgp-example.bulkwalk.zip)

# Prerequisites
## env435:
```
$ pip install pyasn1==0.2.3 pysnmp==4.3.5 pysnmp-mibs
$ pip freeze
ply==3.10
pyasn1==0.2.3
pycryptodome==3.4.6
pysmi==0.1.3
pysnmp==4.3.5
pysnmp-mibs==0.1.6
```
## env439:
```
$ pip install pysnmp pysnmp-mibs
$ pip freeze
ply==3.10
pyasn1==0.3.2
pycryptodome==3.4.6
pysmi==0.1.3
pysnmp==4.3.9
pysnmp-mibs==0.1.6
```

# Running the example script

When I use pysnmp 4.3.5 and pyasn1 0.2.3 the script works as expected:
```
(env435) $ python bgp-example-bulkwalk.py
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80101 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80102 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80103 = peer unconfigured
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80104 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80105 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80106 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80107 = peer unconfigured
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80108 = peer unconfigured
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80109 = peer unconfigured
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a8010a = administrative shutdown
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv6'.0x2a014860000100010000000000000064 = peer unconfigured
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv6'.0x2a014860000100010000000000000065 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv6'.0x2a014860000100010000000000000066 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv6'.0x2a014860000100010000000000000067 = hold time expired
```

But when i use the latest pysnmp 4.3.9 with the latest pyasn1 0.3.2 it throws an exception:
```
(env439) $ python bgp-example-bulkwalk.py
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80101 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80102 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80103 = peer unconfigured
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80104 = hold time expired
CISCO-BGP4-MIB::cbgpPeer2LastErrorTxt.'ipv4'.0xc0a80105 = hold time expired

Some background: https://gist.github.com/XVilka/8346728

In brief: if ``number_of_colors`` is 256, we can pretty safely assume to support emitting 256 colors, and this is currently possible with ``term.color(196)`` or some such. However, as a "formatting name" it is deplorable. It would require using a reference guide to map colors-by-number.  I actually spent some time trying to find a mapping and came up pretty short, I guess you would run a script that emits all 256, pick the one you like, and note it down.

As an API, supporting ``{t.bold_color196}`` is very much against the "easy to write, easy to read" philosophy that Blessings encourages. What follows is my draft proposal.

rgb method
---------------
```
@property
def rgb(self)
   """
   Return a callable accepting arguments ``(red, green, blue)``, which emit a sequence approximated to the colorspace of the terminal bound by :attr:`number_of_colors`. The values of each color are 0-255.
(...)"""
```

This would be exactly like the existing ``color(n)``, except if given ``rgb(255, 50, 100)`` on a terminal where ``number_of_colors`` is 16, this would be "coerced" (or downsampled) as though you called ``rgb(255, 85, 85)`` which is exactly the expected color in the CGA palette, emitting the exact same sequence of ``term.bright_red`` or ``color(12)``.

true_colorspace context manager
---------------------------------------------

```
@contextmanager.contextlib
def true_colorspace():
   """
   Context manager that enables mapping of compound color names and :attr:`rgb` values
   to a True color (24-bit, 16,777,216 colors) colorspace.
   """
```

This is **necessary** as a context manager: we **cannot determine whether the given terminal emulator supports true colors**: There is no ``xterm-16777216color`` terminal name, or anything like it.  There is no situation that I know of where the terminal capability database for ``number_of_colors`` could return 16,777,216. A terminal emulator that supports it provides no method to query whether or not it supports it. For those who know their intended audience/emulator, they may rightly go ahead and use them, or provide configuration to enable it through the use of such context manager.

Compound Formatters as X11 color names
---------------------------------------------------------

Use the official X11 color names, http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X11_color_names as compound formatters. These are mapped by their hexidecimal values to rgb() to emit the appropriate sequence. This allows one to use "deep_pink", which is (255, 20, 147), but on a 16-color terminal it would be mapped to the same sequence as term.bright_red.

`Union` types are equivalent (but not identical) when they differ in the order of arguments. Currently, `type_str` prints out `Union` type with arguments in the same order as it is stored in the type. But I think it might be better (or at least optional with argument) to output it in a canonical and reproducible way (probably just sort it). This would allow one to output descriptions of strings without being surprised by random replacements of types with equivalent types. See here for more information: https://github.com/python/typing/issues/559

So, my suggestion would be to add to `type_str` an argument `canonical` which would always output description with everything in the same order, no matter if you are outputting for equivalent but not identical types.

issue_title: Running a scheduled task if a condition is met within a while loop
issue_body:
I want to run a scheduled task using `schedule` module (https://github.com/dbader/schedule) on the condition that variable=1.

So far, I've written:

    import schedule
    import time
    
    def job1():
      print("123")

    def job2():
      print("456")
    
    while True:
        from otherscript import variable
        if variable==1:
        	print("on")
        	schedule.every().day.at("06:05").do(job1) 
        	schedule.every(10).minutes.do(job2).tag('10mintasks')
        if variable==0:
        	print("off")
        	schedule.clear('10mintasks')

        schedule.run_pending()
        time.sleep(1)

On second thought, I think this might be a wrong way of writing the code, since if `schedule.every()` is in the while loop, then the scheduled task will occur more frequently than 10mins. I think everytime the loop occurs, a new 10mins task is created, causing many 10mins tasks to be generated over time. The `schedule.every()` might thus need to be outside the while loop. This is also reflected in the example in the documentation.


Thus, is there a way I can write conditions for the scheduled 10 mins task to run based on a variable that can change in a while loop?

It was not expected that pos_tags are not ready to be used for lemmatization.
Here is my code:
```
text = "They told us to duck"

blob = TextBlob(text)

for word, pos in blob.pos_tags:

    print("{w}: {p}".format(w=w.lemmatize(pos), p=pos))

So, I catch an error: 
> File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/textblob/decorators.py", line 38, in decorated

issue_title: Unabe to create light theme from gif files
issue_body:
![Image](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/403741067335434240/422527221408989194/scrap.png?width=400&height=271)

This happens on any gif I try. Normal theme creation works fine.

Don't know if this is true for other platforms but on Windows I see different times.

```python
data = {}
for _ in range(1000000):
  bt = psutil.boot_time()
  if bt not in data:
    data[bt] = 0
  data[bt] += 1
```

`{1491482416.0: 870701, 1491482417.0: 129299}`

I thought maybe this value should be cached.  But that would only help to get the same answer within an existing process.  In reality any process on that machine should return the same value right?
If it will only ever be off by 1 *(because of rounding errors or whatever)*... then maybe a better solution would be to always return an even number?

**edit**

Now that I think about it, to return a consistent number it would require you to know the two numbers it's alternating between.  There doesn't seem to be a way to just call boot_time() once and perform some math on it and return a value.  You need to know the two numbers it's alternating between.

issue_title: KafkaProducer hangs on creation inside Openstack Neutron ML2 Plugin
issue_body:
I'm trying to code an Openstack Neutron ML2 Plugin (api.MechanismDriver) that will send events on a Kafka server.

My code works fine when I run it directly inside a python script, but when I add the same code to the ML2 Plugin, the KafkaProducer creation never returns.

This will hang forever when called inside an ML2 Plugin:
``producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers=['my_server_ip'])``

The creation of a Consumer works fine.

I was able to find a workaround by creating a SimpleProducer instead, but as it is marked as deprecated, I would like to change it back to a KafkaProducer.

This works fine:
``client = SimpleClient(KAFKA_SERVERS[0])``
``producer = SimpleProducer(client, async_send=False)``

The ML2 Plugin is a class, with methods that will get called on certain events.  The KafkaProducer would get created when those methods get called.

I understand this is not an Openstack project, but I was wondering if someone would see a major difference between the way the KafkaProducer gets created compared to a KafkaConsumer or SimpleProducer.

Thanks !

_libc = ctypes.cdll.LoadLibrary(ctypes.util.find_library('c') or 'libc.so.6')

this fix seems to work.
can I create a pull request?

thanks

I am implementing schedule object to comply with B-AAC server standard. But I have some trouble with your implementation regarding two specific properties:
```
@register_object_type
class ScheduleObject(Object):
    objectType = 'schedule'
    properties = \
        [
        ...
        , OptionalProperty('weeklySchedule', ArrayOf(DailySchedule))
        , ReadableProperty('priorityForWriting', Unsigned)
        ...
        ]
```

These properties are not normally writable and it is consistent with basic declaration of schedule object in BACnet specification.
But when the server implements internal schedule process, these two properties become writable according to “K.3.2 BIBB - Scheduling-Internal-B (SCHED-I-B) “


In my first test, I try to override the `properties` array from `ScheduleObject`:
```
class MyScheduleObject(ScheduleObject):
    properties = [
        ...
        WritableProperty('weeklySchedule', ArrayOf(DailySchedule)),
        WritableProperty('priorityForWriting', Unsigned)),
        ...
    ]
```
But it doesn't work because `properties` array is not taken into account. The only solution that I found is to change manually the property configuration:
```
self._attr_to_property('priorityForWriting').mutable = True
```

My first question is to know if my analyze is correct ? Maybe I’m missing something.

If my understanding is okay I have a remark regarding robustness. Modifying the internal object property using "dot chain" is not safe because internal change induces an update in high level application too.
That's why I suggest to replace :
```
self._attr_to_property('priorityForWriting').mutable = True
```
by
```
ScheduleObject.update_property('priorityForWriting', optional = False, mutable = True)
```

See attached patch for details about implementation.
[0008_UpdateBehavior.txt](https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/files/1401759/0008_UpdateBehavior.txt)

issue_title: TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'str'
issue_body:
On Python 3, MutatorMath throws an exception `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'str'`. This can be reproduced by running `python objects/mutator.py`, which runs the doctests. Notice that this does not occur on Python 2. 

The exception is raised in `Mutator.getFactors()` when sorting the list of deltas which are tuples of three values: a factor, an object to be compared, and either an axis name or None. Here:

https://github.com/LettError/MutatorMath/blob/63021305eee3ea99421f30fed9f7b210254399e5/Lib/mutatorMath/objects/mutator.py#L239

The exception is due to a change in behavior between Python 2 and 3: the former allows comparing  `str` and `None`, but Python 3 changes this behavior and raises an exception instead. 

A possible fix for this could be to only sort the deltas tuple by the first two tuple items and disregard the last.

issue_title: Namespace declarations on elements are lost during parsing
issue_body:
Currently, `xmltodict` does not return any information about any namespace declarations that may have been present on the elements.

Expat does not treat these as regular attributes (as they are not part of the XML data, and are only considered useful to the XML parser itself) and they are therefore not included as attributes in the returned dictionary from `xmltodict.parse()` either.

To get access to the namespace declarations during Expat parsing, you must provide a "start namespace declaration" handler for Expat to call (ref. [xml.parsers.expat.xmlparser.StartNamespaceDeclHandler](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pyexpat.html#xml.parsers.expat.xmlparser.StartNamespaceDeclHandler)).

Here's a crude example of how `xmltodict` can be modified to include the namespace declarations as a named attribute (`@xmlns`) on the element where they are defined (the value of the attribute is an ordered dictionary of namespaces keyed by their prefix, as defined in the namespace declaration):

```diff
@@ -67,6 +67,7 @@ class _DictSAXHandler(object):
         self.strip_whitespace = strip_whitespace
         self.namespace_separator = namespace_separator
         self.namespaces = namespaces
+        self.namespace_declarations = OrderedDict()
 
     def _build_name(self, full_name):
         if not self.namespaces:
@@ -86,9 +87,15 @@ class _DictSAXHandler(object):
             return attrs
         return self.dict_constructor(zip(attrs[0::2], attrs[1::2]))
 
+    def startNamespaceDecl(self, prefix, uri):
+        self.namespace_declarations[prefix] = uri
+
     def startElement(self, full_name, attrs):
         name = self._build_name(full_name)
         attrs = self._attrs_to_dict(attrs)
+        if attrs and self.namespace_declarations:
+            attrs["xmlns"] = self.namespace_declarations
+            self.namespace_declarations = OrderedDict()
         self.path.append((name, attrs or None))
         if len(self.path) > self.item_depth:
             self.stack.append((self.item, self.data))
@@ -238,6 +245,7 @@ def parse(xml_input, encoding=None, expat=expat, process_namespaces=False,
     except AttributeError:
         # Jython's expat does not support ordered_attributes
         pass
+    parser.StartNamespaceDeclHandler = handler.startNamespaceDecl
     parser.StartElementHandler = handler.startElement
     parser.EndElementHandler = handler.endElement
     parser.CharacterDataHandler = handler.characters
```

The above code works (for my purposes), but it should probably be made somewhat more elegant if to be included in the official `xmltodict` version.

In issue #9 @ilevkivskyi suggests that we should raise an error if any class attribute we're trying to set is already used. This includes `__init__`, `__eq__`, etc. He notes that the typing module raises AttributeError in these cases.

Note that the caller can suppress the generation of `__eq__` by setting `cmp=False`, can suppress `__repr__` by setting `repr=False`, can suppress the generation of `__init__` by setting `init=False`, etc.

I think raising an AttributeError if we try to overwrite a dunder method is a good idea. The caller has the choice to either write their own method and suppress ours, or use ours. But the can't try to do both.

Edit: Fix `init=False` instead of `__init__=False`.

issue_title: For v1.0 some methods of FrozenColor are broken
issue_body:

Heya Kyle,

Love the look of your themes but am having trouble installing them on Windows using Anaconda (2.4.0 64 bit). I'm running this in cmd.exe but I've also tried it using Git Bash (2.7.0 64 bit) with the same result.

    >pip install git+https://github.com/dunovank/jupyter-themes.git
    Collecting git+https://github.com/dunovank/jupyter-themes.git
      Cloning https://github.com/dunovank/jupyter-themes.git to c:\users\matt~1.tre\appdata\local\temp\pip-hmy4xr-build
    Collecting sh (from jupyter-themes==0.1)
      Using cached sh-1.11.tar.gz
        Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
        Traceback (most recent call last):
          File "<string>", line 20, in <module>
          File "c:\users\matt~1.tre\appdata\local\temp\pip-build-v_m6bu\sh\setup.py", line 4, in <module>
            import sh
          File "sh.py", line 37, in <module>
            support." % __version__)
        ImportError: sh 1.11 is currently only supported on linux and osx. please install pbs 0.110 (http://pypi.python.org/pypi/pbs) for windows support.

        ----------------------------------------
    Command "python setup.py egg_info" failed with error code 1 in c:\users\matt~1.tre\appdata\local\temp\pip-build-v_m6bu\sh

But pbs is already installed and appears to be the correct version:

    >pip install pbs
    Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): pbs in c:\anaconda\lib\site-packages

    >pip show pbs
    ---
    Metadata-Version: 2.0
    Name: pbs
    Version: 0.110

I've only take a quick look but here's what seems to be happening...hoping you can shed more light on this!

So, although the [documentation](http://amoffat.github.io/sh/) doesn't mention platform compatibility, the internet seems to suggest ([1](http://www.makeuseof.com/tag/move-shell-scripts-sh-py-awesome/), [2](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28618906/porting-sh-1-11-based-code-to-windows), [3](http://www.blog.pythonlibrary.org/2014/08/07/the-new-sh-package-a-subprocess-wrapper/)) that sh.py doesn't support Windows. It seems that the installer is aborting as soon as it detects that we're trying to install a non-Windows supported module on Windows. 

I think using pbs as an alternative is a red herring; the project is no longer supported. And you'd have to change your code to use pbs anyway.

However, it seems that the only use of sh here is for cp (in `__init__.py`). You're already using shutil - which *is* cross-platform - and also contains a copy function. Would you be able to change to using [shutil.copy](https://docs.python.org/2/library/shutil.html#shutil.copy) instead of sh.cp?

issue_body:

#### This code works
```python
@celery.task(base=QueueOnce, once={'graceful': True})
def slow_task(id):
    sleep(20)
    return "Done!"
```

#### This code doesn't work

@celery.task(base=QueueOnce, once={'graceful': True})

    result = CustomORModel.query.filter_by(id=id).first()

Fresh install, add CustomCountryModel as it is in the example from django-cities, run makemigrations.

issue_title: Create an new event with required_attendes raises an exception
issue_body:
If i want to create an new event, the ```set_xml_value``` method raises an exception. The reason for this exception is the 3rd optional parameter of the Attendee ```last_response_time```. Default value of this parameter is ```None```. The method ```set_xml_value``` checks the instance of that value.

``` python
import sys
import os

sys.path.append(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "libs"))

from exchangelib import DELEGATE
from exchangelib.account import Account
from exchangelib.configuration import Configuration
from exchangelib.ewsdatetime import EWSDateTime, EWSTimeZone
from exchangelib.folders import CalendarItem, Mailbox, Attendee
from exchangelib.services import IdOnly

config = Configuration(username='xxxx', password='xxxx', server='xxxx')
account = Account(primary_smtp_address='xxxx', config=config, autodiscover=False, access_type=DELEGATE)

year, month, day = 2016, 7, 20
tz = EWSTimeZone.timezone('Europe/Copenhagen')

ids = account.calendar.find_items(
    start=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(year, month, day)),
    end=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(year, month, day+1)),
    categories=['Besprechung'],
    shape=IdOnly
)

items = account.calendar.get_items(ids)

required_attendes = items[0].required_attendees

calendar_item = CalendarItem(
        start=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(year, month, day, 14, 30)),
        end=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(year, month, day, 15, 30)),
        subject='Test Termin',
        body='Hello from Python',
        location='devnull',
        categories=['Besprechung'],
        required_attendees=required_attendes
)

res = account.calendar.add_items([calendar_item]) # Raises an exception 

# File "libs/exchangelib/util.py", line 105, in set_xml_value
#     raise AttributeError('Unsupported type %s for value %s on elem %s' % (type(value), value, elem))
# AttributeError: Unsupported type <class 'NoneType'> for value None on elem <Element 't:LastResponseTime' at 0x7fd5684b6728>


```

If an account contains two folders, e.g. `Calendar` and `Kalender`, where `Kalender` is the distinguished folder according to the Exchange server, but `Calendar` has the default name of a distinguished calendar folder, exchangelib returns `Calendar` as `account.calendar` when it should really return `Kalender`.

This issue can be solved by using `dill.source.getname`:

from demo import foo_func as custom_named_func

issue_title: Provide a small cli script to titlecase inputs?
issue_body:
Hi,
It could be helpful to add a small script to this project, maybe called `titlecase`, which basically would put its entries to titlecase:
```
$ titlecase "this is the name of an article.pdf"
This Is the Name of an Article.pdf
```

## A small python script?
```python
from sys import argv
from titlecase import titlecase
if __name__ == '__main__':
    for v in argv[1:]:
        print(titlecase(v))
```
Of course, it could be improved with a documentation, command line arguments etc. (I would suggest using [docopt](https://github.com/docopt/docopt)).

## Or a bash alias?
For instance, I just added [this alias](https://bitbucket.org/lbesson/bin/src/master/.bash_aliases#cl-570) to my Bash profile, and it works fine:
```bash
alias titlecase="python -c \"from sys import argv; from titlecase import titlecase; print(titlecase('\n'.join(argv[1:])))\""
```

issue_title: Make clients able to register for message reply's
issue_body:
It would be useful to not only be able to subscribe to certain commands, but also subscribe to message replies.
For example:
Bot sends: "How many apples do you want?", person replies "3". How does the bot know that the person is replying to his question?
This can be solved by adding a `register_for_reply` function or something similar.

This is how that would look:
```python
def process_reply(message):
    bot.reply_to(message, "Good choice!")

sent_message = bot.send_message(chat_id, "How many apples do you want?", reply_markup=types.ForceReply())
bot.register_for_reply_to(sent_message, process_reply)
```
`register_for_reply_to` would store the sent_message.message_id and process_reply in a dictionary.
When the bot receives a message with `message.reply_to_message.message_id == sent_message.message_id` he notifies `process_reply` and passes the `message` to the function.

I would have implemented this myself, but I will be away for the coming few weeks, so I thought I would just make an issue about this idea :) I hope you get the idea, if not, feel free to ask for clarification.

`def init_file(filepath):
    #
    try:
        if PY3K:

            with open(filepath, 'r', encoding="utf-8") as fhandle:
                contents = re.sub(r'\t', r'  ', fhandle.read())
                contents_split = contents.splitlines()

        else:
            with open(filepath, 'r') as fhandle:
                contents = re.sub(r'\t', r'  ', fhandle.read())
                contents_split = contents.splitlines()
    #

        fixed_flag = detect_fixed_format(contents_split)
        ast_new = process_file(contents_split, True, filepath, fixed_flag)

    else:

issue_title: Clarify inconsistence behavior between normal post releases and post releases of pre-releases
issue_body:
Taken from https://github.com/j0057/setuptools-version-command/pull/3#issuecomment-87577386

`pep440-git` mode current works in the following way:

    1.2.3a-N-ab1234 to 1.2.3aN

That makes a new alpha release *per commit*, while normally projects build towards an alpha release (say, alpha1) and fix number of bugs, then release, then work towards alpha2, fix a number of bugs and release, etc. Current approach leads to builds like "alpha31", "alpha32", etc.

I think normally, the flow would rather be:

    1.2.3a1.dev0 --> 1.2.3a1.devN --> 1.2.3a1
    1.2.3a2.dev0 --> 1.2.3a2.devN --> 1.2.3a2

Current implementation supports also this, so that's not the problem.

However, pre-release handling is a bit inconsistent. For non-pre-releases lib will output post-releases:

    1.2.3-N-abcd123 to 1.2.3.postN

But then, for pre-releases, it increments pre-releases number instead:

    1.2.3a-N-ab1234 to 1.2.3aN

For consistence, I would expect following instead:

    1.2.3a1-N-ab1234 to 1.2.3a1.postN
    1.2.3a2-N-ab1234 to 1.2.3a2.postN

Was this approach chosen based on the pep440 suggestion?

> Creating post-releases of pre-releases is strongly discouraged, as it makes the version identifier difficult to parse for human readers. In general, it is substantially clearer to simply create a new pre-release by incrementing the numeric component.

Two suggestions:

1) Change behavior as suggested here (create post-releases instead of incrementing pre-release number per commit)
2) Document the exception

issue_title: Issues with merge_schedules when list is larger than 2
issue_body:
Firstly thanks for the great project, it's very helpful. I am having issues with `merge_schedules` when the input list is greater than two. The issue seems to be that multiple calls to `pd.merge` happen causing repeated columns, e.g. `market_open_x`, which then fails when the `lambda` function is applied. Here is an illustrative example

```python
import pandas_market_calendars as mcal

start_date = "20170103"
end_date = "20170104"

cme = mcal.get_calendar("CME")
nyse = mcal.get_calendar("NYSE")
ice = mcal.get_calendar("ICE")

s1 = cme.schedule(start_date, end_date)
s2 = nyse.schedule(start_date, end_date)
s3 = ice.schedule(start_date, end_date)

schedules = [s1, s2, s3]

mcal.merge_schedules(schedules, how='inner')

ValueError: ('Can only compare identically-labeled Series objects', 'occurred at index 2017-01-03 00:00:00')
```

As described above, here is an illustration of the internal code causing the
issue

```python
how = "inner"
result = s1
result = result.merge(s2, how=how, right_index=True, left_index=True)
result['market_open'] = result.apply(lambda x: max(x.market_open_x, x.market_open_y), axis=1)
result['market_close'] = result.apply(lambda x: min(x.market_close_x, x.market_close_y), axis=1)
result = result.merge(s3, how=how, right_index=True, left_index=True)
print(result)
```

issue_title: Allow user to skip the best_mask_pattern procedure
issue_body:
Since the low performance described in #124 , it's better to allow user to skip the best_mask_pattern procedure if he/she knows the consequence and is willing to take that risk. My argument is as follows:
1. Faster. Skip finding best mask pattern can reduce 2/3 of total time generating a qrcode. Test:
```
import qrcode
import time

def make():
    qr = qrcode.QRCode(
        version=6,
        error_correction=qrcode.constants.ERROR_CORRECT_H,
        box_size=10,
        border=4,
    )
    qr.add_data('HELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO WORLDHELLO')
    qr.make(fit=True)

    img = qr.make_image()

start = time.time()
for i in range(100):
    make()
end = time.time()
print(end-start)
```
output: 14.734620332717896
And if I comment out [these lines](https://github.com/lincolnloop/python-qrcode/blob/master/qrcode/main.py#L162-L169) to skip the finding of best mask pattern,
output: 5.343664646148682
PS: Using different [penalty rule 1](#125) implementation I wrote.

2. Many other (informal) qrcode generators don't implement any penalty rules at all. And some are super fast.
Like this: [goqr](https://github.com/RaymondChou/goqr), [English fork](https://github.com/loggi/goqr). Written in Go language. The author said that it can *generates 1000 qrcodes in 8s, 8ms/image on everage. Tested under MacBook Pro / i5 2.5G / 4G / Go 1.0.1.* Pretty fast.

3. And if using in Django for generating 50+ qrcodes in one page for a user, CPU will reach 100% during that period(30+ seconds). This is good for DDoS attack.
Best wishes.

issue_title: Change static variables unchanged by constant
issue_body:
Example:

[main.c#L8](https://github.com/facebook/watchman/blob/master/main.c#L8)

This issue is to track adding TCP based listener(s) to the watchman service.
Our model has been to use unix domain sockets where available so that we can dodge some authentication issues.

On Windows, we have a mostly-working layer that uses named pipes, but the model for these is rather different from unix domain sockets, due to the limiting APIs available in various clients.  Debugging on Windows is difficult due to lack of nice tools.

The high level idea to make things more pleasant and easier to work with by removing the named pipe stuff and replacing it with TCP sockets on Windows.  This has the nice side effect that we can also enable the same code on Unix systems and have it tested as part of our integration test suite, and it makes it possible to use comfortable system tracing and profiling tools to figure out problems that arise.

The idea is that we can come up with a simple scheme that looks enough like unix domain sockets that there won't be a massive change to the internals.  Here's the game plan:

* The server creates a regular file in place of the unix domain socket in the filesystem
* We'll refer to this file as the `sockfile` below
* The contents of the `sockfile` describe how to connect to the server over TCP
* Clients can stat the sockpath to determine whether it is a file or a socket and react accordingly (but can short cut that step on Windows where it can only be a file and not a socket)

I've really wanted to avoid any serious authentication code because it is notoriously tricky to get it right and to deal with various authentication schemes.  At this time we still only want to enable local connections to the server, but it is conceivable that we may want to enable some advanced remote/relay use cases with watchman in the future.  With that in mind, we'd like to be able to support more than a very basic authentication scheme.

To allow forwards compatibility, the file that was described above will be a JSON object with the following fields:

* `credentials` - describes the authentication credentials to be used.
* `listeners` - is an array listing the available endpoints

An example might look like this:

```json
{
   "credentials": {
      "username": "myname",
      "password": "secret"
   },
   "listeners": [
      "tcp:[::1]:1234",
      "tcp:127.0.0.1:1234"
   ]
}
```

The above shows how we might indicate that the watchman service is running on port 1234 on both IPv4 and IPv6 loopback addresses, and that the client should use the specified credentials when it connects.

## Accommodating authentication in the watchman protocol

When we start up a listener, we can annotate the default state as being implicit (`EXTERNAL` in the SASL terminology) or whether authentication is needed.  When authentication is needed, the client session will only allow a very limited selection of commands to be used; just those related to setting up an authenticated session.  Once authentication is complete, the whole command set is made available.

We should be able to support SASL rather than narrowly focusing on our immediate need.  That means that the authentication process would go something like:

* Client sends a version command requesting authentication capabilities
* If the server supports authentication, the version command will include a list of configured SASL mechanisms
* If the client is configured to use authentication, it selects the most appropriate mechanism and performs authentication via the new `authenticate` command

## The authenticate command

SASL allows for challenge-response authentication, where the client informs the server about the mechanism that it intends to use, and the server sends some kind of challenge string to initiate the credential exchange, then the client responds with the credential data.  For a simple password scheme, this is a bit chatty, so a more terse form can be used where the client just sends the credentials along with the mechanism.  The two examples are shown below:

```
C:> ["authenticate", {"mech": "PLAIN"}]
S:< {"authenticate": false, "challenge":""}
C:> ["authenticate",{"username":"myname", "password":"secret"}]
S:<{"authenticate": true, "identity":"myname"}
```

This is the preferred, less chatty, version:

```
C:> ["authenticate", {"mech": "PLAIN","username":"myname", "password":"secret"}]
S:<{"authenticate": true, "identity":"myname"}
```

I only really want to support this `PLAIN` use case for now, and to do so without *requiring* the use of something relatively heavyweight, such as libsasl, because I'd like to keep the number of dependencies low enough that we stand a chance of being able to build watchman on Windows without much fuss.

## Configuring credentials

I'd like to avoid maintaining the credentials persistently, so the initial thought is that we'd have the default (and only) mode of operation be that the watchman service would compute a randomized set of credentials when it is initialized and write those to the credentials section of the `sockfile`.  The `sockfile` would be readable only to the owner of the watchman process, which gives it similar properties to unix domain socket access control; the filesystem enforces access.

## What do we need to change and where?

* Plan to remove `stream_win.c`
* Add `stream_tcp.c` or adapt `stream_unix.c` to support TCP
* Add the `authenticate` command
* Add an authenticated status field in the server side watchman_client struct and reject unauthenticated commands
* Modify `listener.c` to bring up the TCP listener based on a global flag that defaults to true on Windows
* Initial TCP listener implementation will randomly assign a port (bind to port `0` and let the kernel assign it) and compute the credentials and generate the `sockfile`.  Future iterations can make this more configurable, but that is not necessary to support Windows.
* pywatchman and fb-watchman (nodejs client) will be updated to test whether the sockfile is a socket or file and adapt accordingly
* pywatchman changes will then need to be synced in to the hgwatchman repo (which may well be in the main mercurial repo by the time we're ready for this)

issue_title: Pinned plone.app.tiles has GenericSetup recursion error
issue_body:
The plone.app.tiles package c.cover is depending on (1.0.1) has a nasty recursion problem in the GenericSetup Profile: it depends on itself in the metadata.xml dependency. 

This causes for example ftw.upgrade to (sometimes) detect a profile dependency infite loop, which disables most of ftw.upgrade in a site where collective.cover is available. This metadata.xml snafoo has been already been fixed in plone.app.tiles 2.X 

According to https://github.com/collective/collective.cover/pull/516  however (where plone.app.blocks is upgrades to 2.X) plone.app.tiles 2.X has backwards incompatible changes. 

If that's too big a change, should / could we ask for a plone.app.tiles 1.0.2 that at least removes just this line ?  @datakurre There's no 1.X maintenance branch for plone.app.tiles . Is it worth the effort or can we help with getting plone.app.tiles 2.X backwards compatible with 1.X so we can upgrade to that ?

issue_title: provide-client-params does not fire for upload_file()
issue_body:
I have followed the [extensibility guide][1] re how to extend boto3 client functionality so that I can omit the `Bucket` argument when calling client methods.

It works with `list_objects`, but not with `upload_file`.

Here's my client factory:

    # s3 module
    def get_client(default_bucket=None, profile_name='default', **kwargs):

        def add_default_bucket(params, **kwargs):
            if 'Bucket' not in params or params['Bucket'] is None:
                params['Bucket'] = default_bucket

        session = boto3.Session(profile_name=profile_name, **kwargs)

        client = session.client('s3')

        if default_bucket is not None:
            event_system = client.meta.events

            event_system.register('provide-client-params.s3.*', add_default_bucket)

        return client

I instantiate a client like so:

    import s3
    s = s3.get_client('some-bucket')

Then `list_objects` works without supplying the ordinarily required `Bucket` param.

    s.list_objects()

But `upload_file` throws an error when I omit the `Bucket` param: `TypeError: upload_file() takes at least 4 arguments (3 given)`:

    s.upload_file(
        Filename='/path/to/file.txt',
        Key='path/to/key.txt',
    )

It does work, however, when I supply `Bucket=None`: 

    s.upload_file(
        Filename='/path/to/file.txt',
        Key='path/to/key.txt',
        Bucket=None
    )

Upload file should not need param `Bucket` with this client because the event handler should insert it.  This seems like a bug but maybe I'm missing something.


  [1]: http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/events.html
  [2]: http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.upload_file

Colander has a [page](http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/colander/en/latest/null_and_drop.html) devoted to describing Colander's use of Null and Drop values. The product's current behaviour doesn't match that description. Specifically, `default` should only affect serialization—but it currently affects deserialization too, *if* `default` is set to `colander.drop`.

Consider this code:

```python
import colander

class Person(colander.MappingSchema):
    name = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.String()
    )
    age = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.Int(),
        validator=colander.Range(0, 200)
    )
    hair_color = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.String(),
        default=u'default brown',
        missing=u'missing brown'
    )

schema = Person()

print('serialized = {}'.format(schema.serialize({
    'name': 'Fred',
    'age': 20
})))

print('deserialized = {}'.format(schema.deserialize({
    'name': 'Fred',
    'age': 20
})))
```

It produces:

```python
serialized = {'age': '20', 'name': u'Fred', 'hair_color': u'default brown'}
deserialized = {'age': 20, 'name': u'Fred', 'hair_color': u'missing brown'}
```

That's exactly what we expect: `'hair_color'` wasn't passed in, so during serialization we got the default value and during deserialization we got the missing value.

Now let's change `default` to `colander.drop`:

```python
import colander

class Person(colander.MappingSchema):
    name = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.String()
    )
    age = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.Int(),
        validator=colander.Range(0, 200)
    )
    hair_color = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.String(),
        default=colander.drop,
        missing=u'missing brown'
    )

schema = Person()

print('serialized = {}'.format(schema.serialize({
    'name': 'Fred',
    'age': 20
})))

print('deserialized = {}'.format(schema.deserialize({
    'name': 'Fred',
    'age': 20
})))
```

It produces: 

```python
serialized = {'age': '20', 'name': u'Fred'}
deserialized = {'age': 20, 'name': u'Fred'}
```

`hair_color` was dropped during serialization—but it was *also* dropped during deserialization. We should have gotten the `missing` value instead.

The problem seems to be that `Mapping.serialize()` and `Mapping.deserialize()` both call `Mapping._impl()`, and `Mapping._impl()` consults `default`. There's a similar problem in `Sequence`: `Sequence.serialize()` and `Sequence.deserialize()` both call `Sequence._impl()`, and `Sequence._impl()` consults `default`. Deserialization should never consult `default`.

At least where mappings are concerned, this is a very old bug. It was introduced in [this commit](https://github.com/Pylons/colander/commit/d15b256ce4a123fc932f720f5a3303c1a700ab91) in 2014. That commit was an attempt to fix [this bug](https://github.com/Pylons/colander/issues/139). That bug actually describes what I'm trying to do: have a  `default` value of `colander.drop` and a `missing` value of `None`. (The `Sequence` bug was introduced in [this commit](https://github.com/Pylons/colander/commit/b5205e03b1701216dea7dd08284694dd34888266) which appears to have copied the `Mapping` code.)

My current workaround is to use `deferred()` and `bind()` to instantiate one variation of the schema for serialization and another one for deserialization.

issue_title: Lag during initialization of teleop and autonomous
issue_body: Our team, 6517, played in the battle for the bluegrass preseason competition yesterday. We had switched to python 2 weeks prior, and it was working out pretty well. When each match began though, our autonomous wouldnt start for about 5 seconds after the autonomous period began, and the same for teleop. Is there a problem with robotpy and pyfrc or do i need to comb through my code?

Here's how I think this will probably work:

* The CameraServer class will live in a separate pip package called robotpy-cameraserver 
* It'll wrap the real cscore with cython, and provide a mechanism to carry the opencv images in any callbacks over to python land, the same way that mjpg-streamer does it
* Because this is python, I think we should launch the camera stuff via multiprocessing -- this will allow the user to upload their camera code via pyfrc's installer
* A requirement that falls out of this is that the cameraserver needs to detect if HAL or WPILib is imported, and if it is to immediately fail (not sure how existing imports work with multiprocessing, this may be tricky to actually do).
* Any image processing callback code will be required to be in its own module, because of the wpilib/hal import restriction

Some unknowns that will need to be addressed (which will need to be addressed for robotpy-ctre as well):

* I only want compilation/cython to happen on the roborio -- though really, I'd rather distribute that as a precompiled wheel package, then when you use pip on a normal system it doesn't do the compilation and simulates it instead
  * An alternative solution might have to be that the wrapper gets built separately, and is included the same way that HAL is included. It would be nice if it were compiled to a real python .so though, instead of needing ctypes... I suspect it would be difficult to share the OpenCV images via ctypes, as the Mat -> ndarray code is [~300 lines of c++](https://github.com/robotpy/mjpg-streamer/blob/master/mjpg-streamer-experimental/plugins/input_opencv/filters/cvfilter_py/conversion.cpp)

issue_title: Wheels built for namespace packages on older setuptools are stuck without PEP-420 support
issue_body: Prior to 28.3.0, the `-nspkg.pth` files would mask the PEP-420 Namespace loader. See pypa/pip#1924. With 28.3.0, setuptools now builds those .pth files to disable the functionality on Python 3.3 and later. However, wheels built using older setuptools will already have those .pth files generated in the wheel so will never have the workaround. As a result, all wheels for all namespace packages need to be (re-)built using setuptools 28.3.0 or later in order to include this functionality.

```
The relevant code is that added to fix https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/issues/93
https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/pull/911

I propose the following patch (at ln1076) along the same lines as the code used elsewhere for the dll files (ln1277):
```
    for name in ['tcl', 'tk']:
        srcdir = src + '/tcl/' + name + libver
        dstdir = dest + '/tcl/' + name + libver
        if os.path.exists(srcdir):
            copyfileordir(srcdir, dstdir, symlink)
        else:
            logger.notify('omitted %s %s because it was not in the source Python distribution', name, libver)
```
This is a clear message and is forward-compatible should the user later elect to install tkinter and re-create the virtual environment.

### Workaround

issue_title: Investigate using PyPy as default python compiler instead of CPython
issue_body:
These days I was reading articles explanning differences between the several derivations of Python world (http://www.toptal.com/python/why-are-there-so-many-pythons, for example) and one of these projects catched my interest.

During my research I read several good things about PyPy, which is not an implementation of Python itself but a JIT (just-in-time) compiler of python code. What really interested me, is that it is able to compile much faster than CPython (the default Python compiler) without many changes in the current code (http://pypy.org/compat.html).

Just look this speed comparison:
http://speed.pypy.org/

According to the benchmarking, code compiled by the current PyPy version is able to run 6.90x faster than code compiled using the default compiler. @breznak also said in ML that his colleague reported that his text processing project was 10x faster using PyPy than CPython.

@scottpurdy suggested we `could try it is with a simple loop that feeds an instance of the CLA classifier. You can then compare speeds for the Python and C++ implementations on both cpython and pypy`.

**Update**

Just to avoid confusion: CPython is not Cython. `cpython` is the our well known Python default compiler (nupic uses the `2.7` version, for example) while `cython` (without the `p`) is a python-like language that use static typing among other features: http://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/23kz8o/cpython_vs_pypy_vs_cython/

The problems with this were reported by @rhyolight when installation went twice through installing numpy, as different versions were required for core(bindings) and nupic.

issue_title: cannot import nupic.bindings.math [OS X 10.10 Homebrew Python]
issue_body:
I'm having problems installing nupic on a new OS X Yosemite mac.
It appears to install correctly but then trying to import the bindings crashes python.

    >>> import nupic.bindings.math
    Fatal Python error: PyThreadState_Get: no current thread
    Abort trap: 6
I'm using homebrew's version of python (v2.7.9), not the system version (v2.7.6).
Here's the link to the mailing list thread http://lists.numenta.org/pipermail/nupic_lists.numenta.org/2015-January/010230.html

Here’s how I installed everything (on a new mac):
 1. I installed XCode from the app store.
 - set ARCHFLAGS="-arch x86_64" in .bash_profile
 - I installed home-brew in the standard way
````ruby -e "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/master/install)"````
 - installed python 2.7.9 `homebrew install python`
 - installed virtualenv `pip install virtualenv`
 - set up a virtual env in called "all" in ~/dev/ `virtualenv all`
 - installed nupic dependencies `brew install automake libtool cmake`
 - activated my virtualenv `source all/bin/activate`
 - installed NuPIC `pip install nupic`

It seemed to install OK. But I get the error above.

 - I have tried cloning the nupic repo and installing from that, but it gave the same error.
 - I also tried cloning the nupic.core repo and installing that as well. But again the same error.
 - Running `which python` outside of a virtualenv gives `/usr/local/bin/python` which is homebrew's python.

#### Some Further Investigating:

I found the point at which python crashes trying to run `import nupic.bindings.math` is when it tries to import a file called `_math.so`. (a copy is in `$NUPIC/nupic/bindings/` after install)
Looking at the console error log thing there is a reference to two versions of python, one is Homebrew's the other is Apple's.
I think the problem is that when _math.so is compiled it's somehow linked to the wrong version of python.

    $ otool -L $NUPIC/nupic/bindings/_math.so 
    ~/dev/nupic/nupic/bindings/_math.so:
        /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1213.0.0)
        /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python (compatibility version 2.7.0, current version 2.7.6)
        /usr/lib/libc++.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 120.0.0)

Looking at the output whilst nupic installs I did spot some references to non Homebrew python.
for example:

    building 'nupic.bindings._math' extension
    clang -fno-strict-aliasing -fno-common -dynamic -I/usr/local/include -I/usr/local/opt/sqlite/include -isysroot /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX10.10.sdk -I/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX10.10.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/Tk.framework/Versions/8.5/Headers -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -arch x86_64 -DNUPIC2 -DNTA_PLATFORM_darwin64 -DNTA_PYTHON_SUPPORT=2.7 -DNTA_INTERNAL -DNTA_ASSERTIONS_ON -DNTA_ASM -DHAVE_CONFIG_H -DBOOST_NO_WREGEX -I~/dev/nupic/external/darwin64/include -I~/dev/nupic/external/common/include -I~/dev/nupic/extensions -I~/dev/nupic -I~/dev/nupic.core/build/release/include -I~/dev/test/bin/../include/python2.7 -I~/dev/test/lib/python2.7/site-packages/numpy/core/include -I/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/include/python2.7 -c extensions/py_support/NumpyVector.cpp -o build/temp.macosx-10.10-x86_64-2.7/extensions/py_support/NumpyVector.o -std=c++11 -m64 -fPIC -fvisibility=hidden -Wall -Wreturn-type -Wunused -Wno-unused-parameter -stdlib=libc++

Apparently -isysroot is set in the python.rb formula for Homebrew:
https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/Library/Formula/python.rb#L213

I've found a few references to similar problems online. Unfortunately none of them have any definitive answers short of replacing the system's python.
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/20003268/impose-linking-to-specific-python-library-on-osx-lion
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22649379/swig-generated-code-links-to-wrong-python-installation
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2020228/swig-python-bindings-for-opencv-2-0-with-macports

issue_title: deposit: advanced record workflow with edit and REST API support
issue_body:
- [ ] Port generic parts of https://github.com/zenodo/zenodo/blob/master/zenodo/modules/deposit/workflows/upload.py to core Invenio by making new generic deposition type similar to (https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/pu/invenio/modules/deposit/types/simplerecord.py)
- [ ] Support for editing records not submitted through deposit

The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: Dev: Have tox produce combined coverage reports
issue_body: See https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.interface/pull/90#pullrequestreview-43303790

Imported from https://projects.linaro.org/browse/QA-1550
"patched" builds is a fundamental aspect of ART-reports. With then, we can compare benchmark results of a build produced from a patch sent to gerrit with a given baseline build, produced from master/trunk. these results are then posted as gerrit review comments on the patch review.

The initial idea to implement this in squad is as follows: every project will have an extra, hidden/private project that is used as a container for those patched builds.The original project is used as baseline, and the patch project can be used to list patches from.

Baseline and patched builds must submit a field that is used to match them together (e.g. the manifest hash we have in ART-reports)

issue_title: 41x slower compression speed for 1 pathological file
issue_body:
For one particular file, I am seeing 41x slower compression speed versus the average for my data set, but only on quality 10/11. This file is a 57MB XML file that has the same small section repeated over and over. It compresses down to 13KB, but takes 72 minutes to process. If I reduce the window to 16 bits, it completes in 45 minutes, and at quality 9 with the default window, it compresses in 1.7s. Decompression takes .125s.

issue_title: [gtk3] Right click not passed to apps
issue_body:
Start an application that is interested in right-click events, such as Midnight Commander. Click with the right button.

Expected: Event sent to the app.

Actual: The menu pops up.

This was indeed not possible with gtk2-based vte. You need to first send the right-click event to VTE, and then depending on whether VTE handled it or not, open up (or not) the menu. You might want to check the modifier keys, e.g. always open the menu on Shift + right-click.

See the [gnome-terminal patch here](https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=13299) for what to do exactly.

In #32 / v1.4.10 a check for sorted build_depend, exec_depend, etc values was introduced. However it does not handle use of the `<depend>` tag well, which is a shortcut for specifying `build_depend`, `build_export_depend` and `exec_depend` for the same package (http://www.ros.org/reps/rep-0140.html#depend-multiple). Apparent from making it easier to read corresponding package.xml files as they become shorter, it is also less prone to forgetting one of the tags.

However, as this tag is (apparently) resolved to build_depend, build_export_depend and exec_depend at loading, the following valid package.xml causes issues with the declaration tag sort check:
```
<build_depend>only_for_compilation</build_depend>

<depend>a_pkg</depend>
<depend>yet_another_pkg</depend>
```
It is impossible to order these three lines to satisfy the check, unless the `<depend>` tags are written as their three underlying tags (i.e. adding another 4 lines).

Short of parsing package.xml in catkin_lint itself or declaring the use of `<depend>` unwanted, I don't know how to solve this issue.

issue_title: Provide an API for modifying memory that side-steps permissions
issue_body:
Currently, using `Cpu` and `Memory` methods for writing memory respects permissions set on memory mappings. This is correct for emulation, but can be a hinderance for API usage and custom analysis. Rewriting read-only memory from a hook is currently not possible without using internal methods, but can be useful to do.

This issue proposes adding either another set of methods to the public API to write memory, or an optional keyword parameter (such as `force` or `ignore_permissions`) to existing methods.

issue_title: Use a custom context object instead of a dict for State.context
issue_body:
Currently, `State.context` is a dict. To access things in the state context, you typically do something like

```
state.context['my_context_key'] = set()
...
state.context['my_context_key'].add(1)
```

Using a str for the key is clunky, and error prone as it can be mistyped. A hack we have throughout the codebase is to save these strings in other variables, a la `state.context[self._context_key_name]` which is also not very clean.

We can create a simple custom class that has a `__dict__` and use this instead of a dict for the storage. 

```
class UserContext(object):
    pass
```

Users will then be able to use the context like a regular python object, which looks a bit nicer. Note that this does not really solve the problem of mistyping an attribute name, there will just be an AttributeError instead of a KeyError.

```
state.context.my_context_key = set()
...
state.context.my_context_key.add(1)
```

issue_title: Allow loading constant parameters in Python API
issue_body: It would be nice to have the equivalent of `dynet::expr::const_parameter()` in the Python interface, perhaps via something like `dynet.parameter(something, update=False)` to match `dynet.lookup()`.

Is it possible to replicate everything in a ComputationGraph to a copy, modify this ComputationGraph for other use, and afterwards copy the replica back to this ComputationGraph?
The motivation for doing this is that when I created two ComputationGraphs, I go the following error 
"Memory allocator assumes only a single ComputationGraph at a time"
"Attempted to create >1 CG"
So it seems that the natural thing to do is somehow save the existing graph somewhere while computing using another graph, and come back to the existing graph after the job with another graph is done. (The two graphs ought to operate on the same instance.)

In the minibatch (documentation)[http://dynet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/minibatch.html], the following line appears in the examples:

```python
scores_sym = dy.softmax(W*dy.concatenate([word_1, word_2])+b)
loss_sym = dy.pickneglogsoftmax(scores_sym, out_label)
```

I came away with this with the impression that soft max has to be performed before `pickneglogsoftmax`, but I had assumed that the soft max operation happens inside the picking function here. Assuming the intuitive thing that this function combines the soft max with the pick, this documentation is somewhat misleading. Can someone confirm that this is a double softmax?

I get several warnings that some reactions/metabolites don't follow the BiGG notation standards.

This seemed strange at first since I only use BiGG reactions to build models. A few problematic cases seem to be:

- metabolites with upper case: "LalaDgluMdap_c", "lipidA_c", etc...
- sink reactions not tagged with DM or SK: "R_sink_bmocogdp_c"

If BiGG models don't pass the BiGG notation tests, maybe these requirements are a bit too stringent ? 

If memote is going to be a community effort, maybe it would be important to check if there is a consensus in the community to enforce these notations.

I think this might be an overkill for most people since, in general, people use a draft model from modelSEED or KEGG as their starting point for curating a model.

issue_title: catching incorrect transfers and provision operations
issue_body:
The following methods need to check if the source wells have either too little volume before the operation and/or the destination volumes have too much volume after the operation.  Otherwise, you can accidentally create negative volumes and overflow wells. 

transfer, consolidate, distribute, stamp

provision, dispense

Here are some helper methods I have used inside of custom versions of these:

`    def _assert_safe_to_pipette_from(self, sources):
        sources = ensure_list(sources)
        if not all([well.volume >= get_well_safe_volume(well) for well in sources]):
            raise Exception('Not safe to pipette from %s'%sources)
        
    def _assert_valid_add_volume(self, dests):
        dests = ensure_list(dests)
        if not all([well.volume <= get_well_max_volume(well) for well in dests]):
            raise Exception('Too much liquid added to dests: %s'%dests)            

    def _assert_valid_transfer(self, sources, dests):
        sources = ensure_list(sources)
        if not all([well.volume >= get_well_dead_volume(well) for well in sources]):
            raise Exception('Too much liquid drawn from sources: %s'%sources)
        self._assert_valid_add_volume(dests) `

issue_title: Should we HTML encode subscription names
issue_body:
@tseaver I tried to use

```python
t = client.topic('foo')
s = t.subscription('bar with space')
```

and got a strange error. Should we forbid subscription names that don't adhere or manually escape them for users? I wrote a docstring edit but wanted to discuss before sending a PR

```diff
diff --git a/gcloud/pubsub/subscription.py b/gcloud/pubsub/subscription.py
index e3da8a0..8c54d39 100644
--- a/gcloud/pubsub/subscription.py
+++ b/gcloud/pubsub/subscription.py
@@ -26,7 +26,10 @@ class Subscription(object):
     https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/reference/rest/v1/projects.subscriptions
 
     :type name: string
-    :param name: the name of the subscription
+    :param name: the name of the subscription. Must start with a letter, and
+                 contain only letters, numbers, dashes, underscores, periods,
+                 tildes, plus or percent signs. It must be between 3 and 255
+                 characters in length, and it must not start with ``goog``.
 
     :type topic: :class:`gcloud.pubsub.topic.Topic`
     :param topic: the topic to which the subscription belongs..
```

Moved over from #910 per @dhermes 

Goal of this issue is to decide:

1. Is it reasonable to provide one-liners to do things like `create_topic()` which is just a wrapper:
  ```python
def create_topic(self, name):
  topic = self.topic(name)
  topic.create()
  return topic
```
1. Can we agree on the naming convention of what does and does not require an API request

---

Comment from the pull request was...

> The name create_topic is ambiguous to new users. Does it mean create a Topic instance or actual insert one?

That's a good point. I think we should adopt the attitude that `client.<verb>_<noun>()` is an API request. So `create_topic` would hit the API trying to 'insert' the topic (using Google's API lingo). `client.<noun>` is nothing more than a factory for the noun which won't fire an API request but gives you back an instance of the <noun>.

> We'd need to offer Client.topic and Client.create_topic side-by-side since users would need to construct pre-existing topics without sending insert.

Totally agree -- I think we had this debate already and I'm totally sold on the need for `.topic()` as the sole way to get a hold of a topic object, without hitting the API (as the topic literally has *zero* extra metadata).

For other APIs, where the object itself holds lots of (potentially important) metadata, I think it might be worthwhile to provide a `get_<noun>()` to pull down that metadata, in addition to the `<noun>()` method which just gives you an object which may not be tied to the remote (authoritative) representation.

An example here might be `bucket`.

- `.bucket()` gives me a bucket object, sans remote data (no API request).
- `create_bucket()` explicitly creates the bucket in the remote service, throwing an error if I was unable to do so.
- `get_bucket()` would be an API request that gives me all the metadata about a bucket.

Note that the `<verb>_bucket()` methods are really just shortcuts so that I can have one-liners for the common things I want to do with a bucket. (ie, `create` == `bucket = client.bucket('name'); bucket.create(); return bucket;`)

In boto for S3, they have this `verify` or `check` parameter on the bucket constructor, so the comparisons look like:

Action|boto|gcloud (suggestion)
---|---|---
Get a bucket, no API request|`connection.get_bucket(validate=False)`|`client.bucket()`
Get a bucket + metadata|`connection.get_bucket()`|`client.get_bucket()`

I personally don't love the boto method, and would much rather the "gcloud (suggestion)" option.

if __name__ == '__main__':

issue_title: Cloud Spanner Client should prevent nested transactions
issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 486182624, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20release%20blocking', 'name': 'status: release blocking', 'color': 'ffee58', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': '8bc34a', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Cloud Spanner does not support [nested transactions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nested_transaction). But the client library does allow users to call `database.run_in_transaction` inside of the callback that is provided to an outer `database.run_in_transaction`. This is misleading since users might believe that this will behave like a nested transaction but in reality these will be two independent transactions. This is confusing and a source of bugs. Specifically the inner transaction might succeed while the outer might ABORT, in which case the callback will be rerun thus rerunning the inner transaction. 
We should prevent this from happening by detecting and raising an error if someone calls `database.run_in_transaction` inside the callback provided to `database.run_in_transaction`.  Note that this needs to be done before Beta since this is a known breaking change.
Also note that if we come across a use case where we do want to allow calling a nested `database.run_in_transaction`, we should be able to enable that in future without making a breaking change. 

cc @jgeewax @bjwatson

issue_body:
Hi,

I am trying to implement a simple task queue using Pub/Sub and I think I am running into a bug with _FlowControl_.

I have a few workers that need to execute a callback one message by one (without concurrency) and I use the _FlowControl_ and `max_messages=1` for that. I am expecting that every worker receive one message, process it, ack it, then pull another one.

```
import time
from google.cloud import pubsub
from google.cloud.pubsub import types

subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()

subscription_name = 'projects/{project_id}/subscriptions/{sub}'.format(
    project_id='my-project-id',
    sub='my-subscription'
)

flow_control = types.FlowControl(max_messages=1)
    
subscription = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_name, flow_control=flow_control)

def callback(message):
    print('Received')    
    time.sleep(5)
    print('Done')
    message.ack()

future = subscription.open(callback)

future.result()
```
But when I publish a few messages using the following code, they are all received and processed concurrently, by a single worker.

```
from google.cloud import pubsub
from google.cloud.pubsub import types

publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()

topic = publisher.topic_path('my-project-id', 'my-topic')

for i in range(1,5):	
	publisher.publish(topic, b'my-message')
```

Am I missing something or is this a bug ?

Thanks !

**My environment details**

- OS: Ubuntu 17.10
- Python: 3.6
- google-cloud-pubsub : 0.30.1
pandas is a natural choice, not necessarily for storage, but as an adaptor

    from pathos.pools import ProcessPool as Pool
ImportError: No module named pools
```

issue_title: `FindInMap` should not allow `If` as a parameter
issue_body:
Given something like:
```python
FindInMap(
    "MyMap",
    Ref("SomeParameter"),
    If('SomeConditional', 'key1', 'key2'),
),
```
When uploading this template to CloudFormation, it results in the following error:

> Template validation error: Template error: every Fn::FindInMap object requires three parameters, the map name, map key and the attribute for return value

In order to be processed successfully, it must be written as:
```python
If(
    'SomeConditional',
    FindInMap(
        "MyMap",
        Ref("SomeParameter"),
        'key1',
    ),
    FindInMap(
        "MyMap",
        Ref("SomeParameter"),
        'key2',
    ),
),
```
This is confirmed by the CF documentation at http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/intrinsic-function-reference-findinmap.html under "Supported Functions", which states that the only functions that can be used inside of `FindInMap` are `Ref` or another `FindInMap`.

It would be nice if Troposphere could provide this "compile time" validation like it does for so many other things.

issue_title: Unreachable (bogus) artifact cache causes build to fail.
issue_body:
I started up a dummy artifact server to do some testing with cache_setup. I wanted to make sure I got a warning if no artifact caches were reachable. Here is the code for that server:

```python
import time
import SimpleHTTPServer
import SocketServer

def get_delayed_handler(delay):
  class DelayResponseHandler(SimpleHTTPServer.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_HEAD(self):
      time.sleep(delay)
      self.send_response(200)
      self.end_headers()
  return DelayResponseHandler

def setup_delayed_server(delay):
	server = SocketServer.TCPServer(("localhost", 9999), get_delayed_handler(delay))
	server.serve_forever()
	return server

timeout = setup_delayed_server(1)
```

issue_title: The SetupPy task uses an installer for `--run` with incorrect mixins.
issue_body: The `SetupPy` task in both new and old python backends uses a custom `SetupPyRunner` installer that fails to require a `setuptools` mixin matching the specified `PythonSetup` `setuptools` requirement. The result is that `setup.py` is executed under the setuptools in the python `Interpreter`'s site-packages if any.
